([明治維新](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%98%8E%E6%B2%BB%E7%B6%AD%E6%96%B0#Japanese "wikt:明治維新"), *Meiji Ishin*; Japanese pronunciation: [[mei.(d)ʑi iꜜ.ɕiɴ, meː-]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")[[1]](#cite_note-1)), referred to at the time as the **Honorable Restoration** (御維新／御一新, *Goi(s)shin*),[[2]](#cite_note-2) also known as the **Meiji Renovation**, **Revolution**, **Regeneration**, **Reform**, or **Renewal**, was a political event that restored [imperial](/wiki/Imperial_House_of_Japan "Imperial House of Japan") rule to [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") in 1868 under [Emperor Meiji](/wiki/Emperor_Meiji "Emperor Meiji") and led to the [westernisation](/wiki/Westernization "Westernization") of Japan. Although there were ruling emperors before the Meiji Restoration, the events restored practical power to, and consolidated the political system under, the [Emperor of Japan](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan").[[a]](#cite_note-4) The Restoration led to enormous changes in Japan's political and social structure and spanned both the late [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period") (often called the [Bakumatsu](/wiki/Bakumatsu "Bakumatsu")) and the beginning of the [Meiji era](/wiki/Meiji_era "Meiji era"), during which time Japan rapidly [industrialised](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization") and adopted [Western ideas](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture"), production methods and technology.

The **Meiji Restoration** ([明治維新](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%98%8E%E6%B2%BB%E7%B6%AD%E6%96%B0#Japanese "wikt:明治維新"), *Meiji Ishin*; Japanese pronunciation: [[mei.(d)ʑi iꜜ.ɕiɴ, meː-]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")[[1]](#cite_note-1)), referred to at the time as the **Honorable Restoration** (御維新／御一新, *Goi(s)shin*),[[2]](#cite_note-2) also known as the **Meiji Renovation**, **Revolution**, **Regeneration**, **Reform**, or **Renewal**, was a political event that restored [imperial](/wiki/Imperial_House_of_Japan "Imperial House of Japan") rule to [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") in 1868 under [Emperor Meiji](/wiki/Emperor_Meiji "Emperor Meiji") and led to the [westernisation](/wiki/Westernization "Westernization") of Japan. Although there were ruling emperors before the Meiji Restoration, the events restored practical power to, and consolidated the political system under, the [Emperor of Japan](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan").[[a]](#cite_note-4) The Restoration led to enormous changes in Japan's political and social structure and spanned both the late [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period") (often called the [Bakumatsu](/wiki/Bakumatsu "Bakumatsu")) and the beginning of the [Meiji era](/wiki/Meiji_era "Meiji era"), during which time Japan rapidly [industrialised](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization") and adopted [Western ideas](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture"), production methods and technology.

The origins of the Restoration lay in economic and political difficulties faced by the [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate"). These problems were compounded by the encroachment of foreign powers in the region which challenged the Tokugawa policy of *[sakoku](/wiki/Sakoku "Sakoku")*, specifically the arrival of the [Perry Expedition](/wiki/Perry_Expedition "Perry Expedition") under orders from United States president [Millard Fillmore](/wiki/Millard_Fillmore "Millard Fillmore"). Under subsequent [unequal treaties](/wiki/Unequal_treaties "Unequal treaties"), Japan was forced to open to the West, questioning the *[shōgun](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dgun "Shōgun")*'s political authority over maintaining Japanese sovereignty. The Emperor's rebuke of shogunal actions led to the emergence of an ideological divide within the *[samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai")* class concerned with their [feudal](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") obligations to both the *shōgun* and the Emperor. Many lower and middle-ranking *samurai* became *[shishi](/wiki/Shishi_(Japan) "Shishi (Japan)")* ("men of spirit") who were committed to the Emperor's proclamations to [expel the barbarians](/wiki/Order_to_expel_barbarians "Order to expel barbarians"). Factional disputes within the [domains](/wiki/Han_system "Han system") led some domains to conflict with the Tokugawa. After some initial setbacks, the domains organised into an anti-Tokugawa alliance, and, led by [Satsuma and Chōshū](/wiki/Satch%C5%8D_Alliance "Satchō Alliance"), they overthrew the shogunal system.

On 3 January 1868, Emperor Meiji declared political power to be restored to the Imperial House. The goals of the restored government were expressed by the new emperor in the [Charter Oath](/wiki/Charter_Oath "Charter Oath"). Subsequent Tokugawa resistance to the new government materialised in the [Boshin War](/wiki/Boshin_War "Boshin War") and the short-lived [Republic of Ezo](/wiki/Republic_of_Ezo "Republic of Ezo"), but by the 1870s, the Emperor's authority was practically unquestioned. The new government reorganised whole strata of society, abolishing the old currency, the domain system, and eventually the class position of the *samurai*.

The abolition of the shogunate and industrialisation of society in [emulation of foreign imperial powers](/wiki/Westernization#Japan "Westernization") led to backlash with the [Saga Rebellion](/wiki/Saga_Rebellion "Saga Rebellion") and the [Satsuma Rebellion](/wiki/Satsuma_Rebellion "Satsuma Rebellion"), but ultimately ended feudalism in Japanese society. The Meiji Restoration was the political process that laid the foundation for the institutions of the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan"), and would have far-reaching consequences in [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") as Japan pursued [colonial](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism") interests against its neighbours. The [Meiji Constitution](/wiki/Meiji_Constitution "Meiji Constitution") of 1889 would remain in place until the [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") [occupation of Japan](/wiki/Occupation_of_Japan "Occupation of Japan") after the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

### Political and social structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Political and social structure")]

In the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period"), Japan was governed by a strict and rigid social order with inherited position. This hierarchy in descending order had the [Emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan") and their [Court](/wiki/Imperial_Court_in_Kyoto "Imperial Court in Kyoto") at the top. The *[shōgun](/wiki/Shogun "Shogun")*, with the *[rōjū](/wiki/R%C5%8Dj%C5%AB "Rōjū")* and *[daimyō](/wiki/Daimyo "Daimyo")* below him inhabited the upper strata of society. Below them were various subdivisions of *[samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai")*, farmers, artisans, and merchants.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197222-5) Historian [Marius B. Jansen](/wiki/Marius_B._Jansen "Marius B. Jansen") refers to the political organisation of the system as being one of "[feudal](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") autonomy".[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen19943-6) This was a structure of government where the *shōgun* was granted extensive control to the various *daimyō* over their own [domains](/wiki/Han_system "Han system") to control their own jurisdiction while paying homage to him through irregular taxation, the seeking of permission for marriage and movement,[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen19946-7) and systems such as that of [alternate attendance](/wiki/Sankin-k%C5%8Dtai "Sankin-kōtai").[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197217–22-8) The total population of *samurai* families in the 19th century numbered around 5–6% of 30 million people (1,500,000–1,800,000), among these families, roughly 1 in 50 was an "upper *samurai*" while the rest were divided mostly evenly between "middle" and "lower" *samurai*, with each division containing more subdivisions.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197224,_27-9)

The influence exerted by the [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate") (*bakufu*) in contemporary Japan was built on the distribution and management of land. Split into domains, each domain was measured by *[koku](/wiki/Koku "Koku")*, or the amount of rice a given area of land could produce per annum. By 1650, the *shōgun* directly controlled land producing roughly 4.2 million *koku* of rice, with his direct retainers, other members of the Tokugawa family (*[shinpan daimyō](/wiki/Shinpan_(daimyo) "Shinpan (daimyo)")*), and his vassals (*[fudai daimyō](/wiki/Fudai_daimy%C5%8D "Fudai daimyō")*) controlling a combined total of land producing 12.9 million *koku* out of a national 26 million *koku*. The remaining 9.8 million *koku* (just under 38%) was parceled out between about 100 rival *[tozama daimyō](/wiki/Tozama_daimy%C5%8D "Tozama daimyō")*, the descendants of those who had fought against the Tokugawa at the [Battle of Sekigahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara"). Many of the strongest *tozama* domains were located in western Japan away from centres of power, with the *fudai* often controlling government offices, but with smaller provinces to incentivize them to preserve the system.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197215–17-10)

According to the [Neo-Confucian](/wiki/Edo_neo-Confucianism "Edo neo-Confucianism") tenets of *bakufu* authority, the system of hierarchy developed in *[bushidō](/wiki/Bushid%C5%8D "Bushidō")* encouraged a series of descending subordinations,[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197234–35-11) but unlike in China, its adoption by the *bakufu* came to inform an ethic that was distinct from the structural organisation of the Tokugawa state; this allowed it to coexist with Western scientific methods. Historian [William G. Beasley](/wiki/William_G._Beasley "William G. Beasley") argues that there was a tension between this official state ideology that encouraged enlightened meritocratic rule and the rigid class structure that prevented the lower and middle ranking *samurai* bureaucrats from advancing their position.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197238-12) When exacerbated by foreign and domestic crises, and in spite of official attempts to begin promoting *samurai* to offices beyond their inherited position,[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197261–63-13) the social bonds between these systems weakened, leading to reformist and revolutionary attitudes among the *samurai*.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197239–40-14) Merchant classes, that had been flourishing economically in developing mass culture and [communication](/wiki/Mass_communication "Mass communication"), were forbidden to translate their influence into political power.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwale200921-15)

### Ideological currents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ideological currents")]

Beginning especially in the last quarter of the 18th century, a kind of [Shintō revival](/wiki/History_of_Shinto#Restoration_Shinto_and_Late_Mito_Studies "History of Shinto") was occurring alongside a growing interest in [Dutch studies](/wiki/Rangaku "Rangaku"). Both schools occurred as part of a turn away from China as the centre of intellectual thought. But adherents to both movements were careful not to assert that their learning was in any way meant to upset the established political order.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197279-16) The work of the [Mito School](/wiki/Mitogaku "Mitogaku") working primarily from the *[Kōdōkan](/wiki/K%C5%8Dd%C5%8Dkan_(Mito) "Kōdōkan (Mito)")*, was especially important in the development of [nativist](/wiki/Nativism_(politics) "Nativism (politics)") concepts connected to *[kokugaku](/wiki/Kokugaku "Kokugaku")* (national learning).[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197282–83-17)

There were limits. When [Hirata Atsutane](/wiki/Hirata_Atsutane "Hirata Atsutane") went too far for the *bakufu* in reviving the political claims the Emperor held, he was silenced.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen199452-18) Still, the development of national learning continued in the Mito School, with the use of the term *[fukoku kyōhei](/wiki/Fukoku_ky%C5%8Dhei "Fukoku kyōhei")* (rich nation, strong army) to describe the solution to domestic and foreign threats applied by the scholar [Fujita Yūkoku](/wiki/Fujita_Y%C5%ABkoku "Fujita Yūkoku").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197282–83-17) Fujita's son, [Fujita Tōko](/wiki/Fujita_T%C5%8Dko "Fujita Tōko"), writing in the wake of news of British victory in the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War") insisted that a desirable position for Japan to take would be *jōi* (repelling the barbarian) to be followed with *kaikoku*, an opening of the country on equal footing.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197284–85-19) When faced with the immediate danger the foreign threat possessed, the work of *kokugaku* scholars led to an evaluation of what was national, which focused attention on the Emperor and [Shintō](/wiki/Shint%C5%8D "Shintō").[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen199453-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/SakumaShozan.jpg/250px-SakumaShozan.jpg)](/wiki/File:SakumaShozan.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Yoshida_Shoin.jpg/250px-Yoshida_Shoin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yoshida_Shoin.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Kaishu_Katsu_1.jpg/250px-Kaishu_Katsu_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kaishu_Katsu_1.jpg)

[Sakuma Shōzan](/wiki/Sakuma_Sh%C5%8Dzan "Sakuma Shōzan") and his pupils [Yoshida Shōin](/wiki/Yoshida_Sh%C5%8Din "Yoshida Shōin") and [Katsu Kaishū](/wiki/Katsu_Kaish%C5%AB "Katsu Kaishū")

[Sakuma Shōzan](/wiki/Sakuma_Sh%C5%8Dzan "Sakuma Shōzan") was a midranking samurai under the *daimyō* [Sanada Yukinori](/wiki/Sanada_Yukinori "Sanada Yukinori") of [Matsushiro Domain](/wiki/Matsushiro_Domain "Matsushiro Domain"), he held a conservative attitude to the social development of Japanese society, but was practical in his approach to the adoption of Western technology. He supplemented his view of Neo-Confucian ethics with that of the adoption of foreign scientific methods,[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197280–82-21) coining the phrase "Eastern ethics, Western science".[[b]](#cite_note-22)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197286-23) In addition to writing about the need for coastal defense, he took charge over cannon [founding](/wiki/Casting "Casting"), built his own camera, and wrote a Japanese-English dictionary designed to contribute to the defense of Japan.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197280–82-21) He opened a school in [Edo](/wiki/Edo "Edo"), teaching over 5,000 students from all over the country.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000288-24) His efforts to promote men of talent (who would be drawn exclusively from the samurai class), and reorganise the Japanese military were incredibly influential among his disciples, not least [Katsu Kaishū](/wiki/Katsu_Kaish%C5%AB "Katsu Kaishū") and [Yoshida Shōin](/wiki/Yoshida_Sh%C5%8Din "Yoshida Shōin"). However, they would serve as the foundation for proposals that would change the social order he was attempting to preserve.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197280–82-21)

### Economic development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Economic development")]

The [Tenpō Reforms](/wiki/Tenp%C5%8D_Reforms "Tenpō Reforms") (1841–1843) were a series of readjustments to government policy designed to reform issues in political and economic organisation, the result of which was to reveal a deep divide between the shogunate and the *daimyō*. During the 1830s and 1840s famine and popular unrest was widespread (e.g. the [Osaka revolt](/wiki/%C5%8Cshio_Heihachir%C5%8D#The_rebellion "Ōshio Heihachirō")),[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000247-248-25) means to resolve these issues and their symptoms had mixed effects.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000251-26) Initial efforts by senior *Rōjū* [Mizuno Tadakuni](/wiki/Mizuno_Tadakuni "Mizuno Tadakuni") involved introducing [sumptuary laws](/wiki/Sumptuary_laws "Sumptuary laws") on the promotion of austerity and limiting consumption.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197264–65-27) Lowering or confiscating the stipends of *samurai* retainers alienated them from serving their lords, or even from the social class altogether with some choosing to pursue personal liberty and mercantile freedom not afforded to them due to their expectations as members of the *samurai* class. *Daimyō* cancelled debt owed to their own merchants and renegotiated favourable terms for debt owed to merchants under *bakufu* jurisdiction—due to resulting higher interest rates imposed on the *daimyō* to borrow more money, across Japan efforts were made to increase domain income.[[c]](#cite_note-29)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000251-26)

Fixed rice [stipends](/wiki/Stipend "Stipend") made the *samurai* vulnerable to fluctuations in the market, periodic coinage [debasement](/wiki/Debasement "Debasement"), and the need to transfer this stipend into the new monetary system. For instance, merchants who acted as agents to sell their stipends often pocketed the profit made on these sales. Meanwhile, as part of the development of urban life, artisans and farmers diversified the production of goods and crops which, due to increasing demand, often drained the *samurai*'s resources while other crops became more lucrative to sell than rice. Many *samurai* were often constantly in debt, with the *daimyō* living under a system of enforced expenditure by the *bakufu* to carry out (among other things) the alternate exchange system and public works projects.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197244–46-30)

The *bakufu* started to show a small annual gold deficit by 1800, which had grown to over half a million *[ryō](/wiki/Ry%C5%8D "Ryō")* by 1837. To recoup some of these losses, the *bakufu* initiated a system of forced loans on the *daimyō* and conferred special privileges to merchants.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197251,_53-31) The resultant diversification of merchant and artisanal industries also brought about a high degree of commercial specialisation and profound changes to rural life. Merchants collectively set up [guilds](/wiki/Za_(guilds) "Za (guilds)") and organised [monopolies](/wiki/Monopolies "Monopolies") over goods and services, obtaining official status as contractors and suppliers.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197243–44-32) By the time of the Tenpō Reforms some of these guilds were broken up as they were believed to be increasing prices, but much of the cause was due to production not keeping up with demand.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197265-33)

The changing economic history of the Edo period drastically altered the traditionally rigid social hierarchy of Tokugawa Japan, with new land becoming available for cultivation and new outlets for commercial trade and manufacturing. Beyond changing the nature of value in local economies, these changes brought with them an erosion of the official class system, with some domains offering the sale of *samurai* status, and many rich commoners educating their children and bribing their way into adoption by poor *samurai* families.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197231–34-34) While the taxation and control over monopolies partially resolved the issue of government finance, it didn't resolve the issue of *samurai* poverty. Among farmers, the ability to increase land cultivation and cope with price fluctuations exacerbated wealth disparities, the difference in the tax burden between different crops meant that many fell into debt and [tenancy](/wiki/Tenant_farming "Tenant farming") to their wealthier neighbours. [Satō Nobuhiro](/wiki/Sat%C5%8D_Nobuhiro "Satō Nobuhiro") claimed that by 1827, at least 30% of farmers had lost land this way.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197255–56-35)

### Foreign influence, 1633–1854

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Foreign influence, 1633–1854")]

Since 1633, a system of national isolation known as *[sakoku](/wiki/Sakoku "Sakoku")* had been imposed by the Tokugawa shogunate, wherein no person was allowed to enter or leave Japan without permission from the *shōgun*. The resultant isolationism fuelled systemic diversification of the domestic economy to fulfil local needs. This socio-cultural development combined with strict regulation and censorship on the topic of politics created a "seldom penetrated" lack of international consciousness.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000257-36) Following the [Shimabara Rebellion](/wiki/Shimabara_Rebellion "Shimabara Rebellion"), the non-Catholic [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") had been permitted to maintain a monopoly through their [factory](/wiki/Factory_(trading_post) "Factory (trading post)") on [Dejima](/wiki/Dejima "Dejima"), just off the coast of [Nagasaki](/wiki/Nagasaki "Nagasaki") in [Saga Domain](/wiki/Saga_Domain "Saga Domain").[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen19948-37) This trade, although limited in scope, had led to a growing understanding of the West by Tokugawa intellectuals.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000257-36) While this limited trade with the Dutch had disseminated aspects of Western culture into Japanese society, it also created an uneven understanding of the development of Western technology and military capabilities that did not seem to threaten the socio-political order.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwale200921-15)

#### Russian Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Russian Empire")]

In the Edo period, a number of incidents occurred where Russians came into contact with Japanese people, due to exploration east by Russia and north by Japan. In 1804, [Nikolai Rezanov](/wiki/Nikolai_Rezanov "Nikolai Rezanov") entered Nagasaki bay with a letter from [Tsar Alexander I](/wiki/Tsar_Alexander_I "Tsar Alexander I") requesting trade. After being refused this request, he staged a series of raids on Japanese settlements in [Sakhalin](/wiki/Sakhalin "Sakhalin") and the [Kuril Islands](/wiki/Kuril_Islands "Kuril Islands") in 1806–1807. The 1811 [Golovnin Incident](/wiki/Golovnin_Incident "Golovnin Incident") inflamed Japanese mistrust of Russian expeditions, until Golovnin explained that the earlier raids by Rezanov had not been sanctioned by the Tsar. The growing contact with [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") caused the *bakufu* to attend to the defense of the northern frontier. The [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") drew the attention of both the Russians and the Japanese, who were concerned that the situation in Europe would impact trade with the Dutch. As a result, Russian interest in Pacific exploration declined until the 1840s.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000260–262-38)

#### Western Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Western Europe")]

During the Napoleonic Wars, the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") assumed control over Dutch colonial possessions in south-east Asia, while Dejima was supplied by neutral [charter ships](/wiki/Chartering_(shipping) "Chartering (shipping)") from other countries.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000264–265-39) In 1808, a British frigate [entered Nagasaki harbour](/wiki/HMS_Phaeton_(1782)#Nagasaki_Harbour_Incident "HMS Phaeton (1782)") demanding supplies from the Dutch.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197277-40) The Dutch station chief was attempting to conceal the situation in Europe, following political crisis in the Netherlands. British lieutenant-governor [Stamford Raffles](/wiki/Stamford_Raffles "Stamford Raffles") assumed [control over Java](/wiki/French_and_British_interregnum_in_the_Dutch_East_Indies "French and British interregnum in the Dutch East Indies"), and made an unsuccessful attempt to bring the Nagasaki trade under British possession.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000264–265-39)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197277-40) This increasing contact with Europe caused the *bakufu* to begin hiring their own specialists to improve their understanding of western languages and cultures.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000265–266-41) However, by the time of the [Morrison Incident](/wiki/Morrison_Incident "Morrison Incident") (1837) and subsequent [suppression of Western scholarship](/wiki/Bansha_no_goku "Bansha no goku") in 1839, the *bakufu* signalled a reverse course.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000267-42)

The threat of the Western powers became far more pronounced when news of Britain's success in the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War") against the [Qing Dynasty](/wiki/Qing_Dynasty "Qing Dynasty") reached Japan. The [Nanking Treaty](/wiki/Nanking_Treaty "Nanking Treaty") (1842) specified the transfer of Hong Kong to British control and detailed the opening of treaty ports. The ideological commitment to [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade") by the British amounted to a loss of Chinese sovereignty over conducting their own foreign affairs.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000270-43) Information about the War was supposed to be strictly controlled, but the information spread among the *daimyō*.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwale200926-44) China's defeat was taken as a severe military and cultural disaster. The responses from officials within the *bakufu* were varied and contradictory; Dutch studies were still viewed with suspicion, but military applications were taken more seriously as interest among *samurai* scholars grew.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwale200927–28-45)

From 1843 reports started to circulate of British and [French](/wiki/July_Monarchy "July Monarchy") interests in the [Ryukyu Islands](/wiki/Ryukyu_Islands "Ryukyu Islands"), then in 1844 the Dutch King [William II](/wiki/William_II_of_the_Netherlands "William II of the Netherlands") sent a letter to Japanese officials advising them to take the initiative in their interactions with foreign powers.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197278-46) It was around this time that the *samurai* intellectual Sakuma Shōzan wrote his first memoranda on the importance of coastal defence.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000271-47)[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197281-48) A planned British expedition approved in 1845 failed to materialise when,[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197278-46) conscious of the infamy incurred by forcing China to buy British opium, and in consideration of the more modest trading opportunities available in Japan, Britain decided to support and capitalise on the success of a planned American expedition to open Japan's ports.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000274-49)

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: United States")]

Main article: [Perry Expedition](/wiki/Perry_Expedition "Perry Expedition")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Japanese_1854_print_Commodore_Perry.jpg/500px-Japanese_1854_print_Commodore_Perry.jpg)](/wiki/File:Japanese_1854_print_Commodore_Perry.jpg)

A Japanese print depicting the arrival of Perry's fleet (1854)

Appointed to command the American expedition in 1852,[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197288-50) [Commodore](/wiki/Commodore_(United_States) "Commodore (United States)") [Matthew C. Perry](/wiki/Matthew_C._Perry "Matthew C. Perry") was initially reluctant to take the command.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000274–275-51) American interests in Japan derived from ambitions to capitalise on the China trade and to ensure the protection of shipwrecked seamen, especially those of the essential and lucrative [whaling](/wiki/Whaling_in_the_United_States "Whaling in the United States") industry. Their goal was to set up ports of free trade, and a place to secure provisions, including a coal pit for steam-ships.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197288-50)[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000274–275-51) The first visit took Perry to [Uraga, Kanagawa](/wiki/Uraga,_Kanagawa "Uraga, Kanagawa"), where after entering [Tokyo Bay](/wiki/Tokyo_Bay "Tokyo Bay"), his consultations with the governor of Uraga led to him handing over documents requesting an end to Japanese isolation. This exchange took place in a ceremony at [Kurihama](/wiki/Kurihama "Kurihama") on 14 July.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197289-52)

Beginning in August 1853, senior *Rōjū* [Abe Masahiro](/wiki/Abe_Masahiro "Abe Masahiro") took the unprecedented step of seeking a mandate from the *daimyō*. The results were inconclusive, with important figures urging different actions. Future *rōju* [Hotta Masayoshi](/wiki/Hotta_Masayoshi "Hotta Masayoshi"); *fudai* lord [Ii Naosuke](/wiki/Ii_Naosuke "Ii Naosuke"); and [Shimazu Nariakira](/wiki/Shimazu_Nariakira "Shimazu Nariakira") of [Satsuma Domain](/wiki/Satsuma_Domain "Satsuma Domain") agreed to some level of compromise, if only temporarily. [Yamauchi Toyoshige](/wiki/Yamauchi_Toyoshige "Yamauchi Toyoshige") recommended rejecting the treaty, while enlisting Dutch specialists to assist in manufacturing weapons. [Tokugawa Nariaki](/wiki/Tokugawa_Nariaki "Tokugawa Nariaki") gave the expulsionist view of the Mito School.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197290–92-53)

Less than a year later Perry returned in threatening [large warships](/wiki/Black_Ships "Black Ships") to conclude the treaty.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunt2016712–713-54) His return in February was sooner than expected, partly because he had heard of a Russian mission in Nagasaki also seeking to negotiate a treaty. The *bakufu* attempted to conduct the talks at Uraga due to its further distance from Edo, but Perry insisted on [Kanagawa](/wiki/Kanagawa "Kanagawa"). The talks were conducted between Perry and [Hayashi Akira](/wiki/Hayashi_Akira "Hayashi Akira") for 23 days.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000278-55) In 1854, the [Convention of Kanagawa](/wiki/Convention_of_Kanagawa "Convention of Kanagawa") was signed. It opened up two treaty ports (Shimoda and [Hakodate](/wiki/Hakodate "Hakodate")), ensured the safety of American seamen, and gave permission for American ships to buy their own provisions. As part of the treaty, [Townsend Harris](/wiki/Townsend_Harris "Townsend Harris") was appointed the first American consul to Japan.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000278–279-56) The treaty excluded any mention of the right to trade, which was considered a positive outcome by the *bakufu*'s negotiators. However, Japan was now in a position where it had to sign similar treaties with Britain and Russia, effectively ending Japanese isolationism.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197296–97-57)

## End of the Tokugawa shogunate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: End of the Tokugawa shogunate")]

Main article: [Bakumatsu](/wiki/Bakumatsu "Bakumatsu")

### Reactions to the Unequal Treaties, 1854–1858

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Reactions to the Unequal Treaties, 1854–1858")]

[!["Revere the Emperor, expel the barbarians" was a four-character idiom popular during the time period.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Yokohama-Sumo-Wrestler-Defeating-a-Foreigner-1861-Ipposai-Yoshifuji.png/250px-Yokohama-Sumo-Wrestler-Defeating-a-Foreigner-1861-Ipposai-Yoshifuji.png)](/wiki/File:Yokohama-Sumo-Wrestler-Defeating-a-Foreigner-1861-Ipposai-Yoshifuji.png)

An 1861 print depicting *[jōi](/wiki/J%C5%8Di "Jōi")* ("expel the barbarians").

The anti-treaty faction was horrified at the extent of the concessions made by Abe Masahiro to Perry in the Convention of Kanagawa. Even reformers who had advocated for compromise were upset at the magnitude of concessions made.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197296–96-58) From the Mito School, Fujita Yūkoku's disciple [Aizawa Seishisai](/wiki/Aizawa_Seishisai "Aizawa Seishisai") extended his teacher's ideas, writing the *Shinron* ("New Theses") in 1825.[[d]](#cite_note-60) Advocating a will to resist, Aizawa believed a policy of *[sonnō jōi](/wiki/Sonn%C5%8D_j%C5%8Di "Sonnō jōi")* ("revere the Emperor, expel the barbarian") would create a unity and resolution among the people. The *shōgun* would be responsible for subordinating the interests of the Tokugawa family to those of the Japanese people, by revering the Emperor as a symbol of the *[kokutai](/wiki/Kokutai "Kokutai")* or national polity.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197283–84-61)

The positions of opening Japan and taking up arms against foreign powers were not mutually exclusive, and believers both in *jōi* and *kaikoku* realised the advantages of Western technology for the purpose of repelling the foreigners.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen199453–54-62) In 1855, Abe resigned as senior *rōju* and was replaced by Hotta Masayoshi.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000283-63)[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197299-64) An outcome of this decision was the estrangement of Tokugawa Nariaki from the senior council, as Hotta continued to drive in a reformist direction.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972100-65)[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen199458-66)

In 1855, the [Nagasaki Naval Training Centre](/wiki/Nagasaki_Naval_Training_Center "Nagasaki Naval Training Center") was founded with Katsu Kaishū serving in an important administrative function, and the individual domains were encouraged to build their own shipyards.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197299,_123-67) Japan reluctantly expanded its treaties to [France](/wiki/Second_French_Empire "Second French Empire"), Britain, the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and Russia. Negotiating treaties with Dutch Commissioner [Donker Curtius](/wiki/Donker_Curtius "Donker Curtius") in 1856 and 1857, a trade agreement was reached that opened Nagasaki and Hakodate to free trade open to all merchants; import duties of 35% were levied on private goods, but it marked a considerable change from the prior Dutch enclave on Dejima.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972104-68)

Harris' arrival in Japan as American consul heralded new treaty negotiations. Leveraging his position by instilling a fear of British and French imperialism (who were in the process of fighting the [Second Opium War](/wiki/Second_Opium_War "Second Opium War")), he pursued negotiations with Hotta, and in 1857 was granted an audience with *shōgun* [Tokugawa Iesada](/wiki/Tokugawa_Iesada "Tokugawa Iesada").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000283–284-69) In 1858, the [Treaty of Amity and Commerce](/wiki/Treaty_of_Amity_and_Commerce_(United_States-Japan) "Treaty of Amity and Commerce (United States-Japan)") was confirmed, with provisions allowing for ambassadorial residence in Edo, [extraterritoriality](/wiki/Extraterritoriality "Extraterritoriality"), the toleration of Christianity, and the opening of five ports for free trade between 1859 and 1863.[[e]](#cite_note-70)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972107–108-71) These treaties have become known as the [unequal treaties](/wiki/Unequal_treaties "Unequal treaties").[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley197298-72)

With the provisions of [the treaties](/wiki/Ansei_Treaties "Ansei Treaties") unpopular among both reformist and reactionary *daimyō*, Hotta sought to silence critics by seeking the approval of [Emperor Kōmei](/wiki/Emperor_K%C5%8Dmei "Emperor Kōmei"), the outcome of which he considered a certainty. However, [members of the Court](/wiki/Kuge "Kuge") were themselves influenced by Mito School writings and by the time Hotta reached [Kyōto](/wiki/Ky%C5%8Dto "Kyōto"), they had been directly petitioned by anti-treaty *daimyō*, including Tokugawa Nariaki. The Emperor and his court expressed disfavour with the Harris Treaty, passing a resolution that encouraged Hotta to once more consult with the *daimyō*. This open rebuff caused Hotta's downfall.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972112–114-73)[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000284–285-74) Although Hotta was forced from office, *bakufu* policy remained the same, and on 4 June 1858, Ii Naosuke was appointed *[tairō](/wiki/Tair%C5%8D "Tairō")*.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972114-75) Ii was conscious of going against the wishes of the Emperor and his Court, but decided to approve the treaty regardless, and it was signed on 29 July.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972114-75) Then, *shōgun* Tokugawa Iesada died on 14 August. Prior to his death it was stipulated that he would be succeeded by [Tokugawa Iemochi](/wiki/Tokugawa_Iemochi "Tokugawa Iemochi"), a child of twelve years old.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972115-76) It was an appointment that had been contested between factions of the *daimyō*, with Tokugawa Nariaki favouring his own son [Tokugawa Yoshinobu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Yoshinobu "Tokugawa Yoshinobu") as candidate for *shōgun*.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000285-77) In 1860, the Treaty of Amity and Commerce was ratified during the [Japanese Embassy to the United States](/wiki/Japanese_Embassy_to_the_United_States "Japanese Embassy to the United States").

### Political violence, 1858–1864

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Political violence, 1858–1864")]

Aware of how unpopular the treaty was, Ii Naosuke took his prerogative to be the restoration of strong, centralised *bakufu* governance. In pursuit of this aim, he enacted the [Ansei Purge](/wiki/Ansei_Purge "Ansei Purge") wherein over one hundred political enemies were punished, with eight killed, and many more were forced into [house arrest](/wiki/House_arrest "House arrest"). This was a use of force by the *bakufu* that had not been seen in centuries.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000285–286-78) His success in recentring political authority with the *bakufu* culminated in [Manabe Akikatsu](/wiki/Manabe_Akikatsu "Manabe Akikatsu") successfully petitioning for retrospective imperial support for the treaties from Emperor Kōmei.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972138–139-79) The principle ideology of conservative reform, *[kōbu gattai](/wiki/K%C5%8Dbu_gattai "Kōbu gattai")*, referenced the 'unity of Court and *bakufu*',[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972120-80) and was an ideological element committed to softly renegotiating the political relationship between the Tokugawa, the Emperor, and the *daimyō*; as part of this relationship, Tokugawa Iemochi was arranged to marry the Emperor's sister, [Kazunomiya](/wiki/Kazunomiya "Kazunomiya").[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000174,_177-81)

As Ii continued to centralise power around the shogunate, one of the people caught in his purges was the *samurai* intellectual Yoshida Shōin. A pupil of Sakuma Shōzan, Yoshida was what came to be known as a *[shishi](/wiki/Shishi_(Japan) "Shishi (Japan)")*, a "man of spirit".[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972147-82) The *shishi* were low and middle-ranking *samurai* who were filled with reverence for the Imperial Court at Kyōto in which lay the essential quality of national purity.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen199499-83) This was an ideological fusion based on Mito School *kokugaku* studies of Neo-Confucian principles of loyalty and the Shintō revival of the early 19th century, with *shishi* believing loyalty to the Emperor to be of the utmost importance.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen199495–96-84) They saw the *bakufu* as becoming increasingly self-interested, with the *daimyō* unwilling to intervene in mediating open disagreement between the Imperial Court and the *bakufu* over the issue of the foreign threat. The crisis of 1858 had changed Yoshida's perspective, where before he had been open to conciliation between the Court and *bakufu*, he now believed the *shishi* should take direct action in response to the actions of the *bakufu* and *daimyō*. Yoshida's increasingly extreme teachings lost him the support of influential *samurai*, as well as his own pupils (including [Kido Takayoshi](/wiki/Kido_Takayoshi "Kido Takayoshi") and [Takasugi Shinsaku](/wiki/Takasugi_Shinsaku "Takasugi Shinsaku")).[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972149–150-85) He was executed in 1859 for planning the assassination of Manabe Akikatsu.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000293-86)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Arimura_Jisaemon.jpg/250px-Arimura_Jisaemon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arimura_Jisaemon.jpg)

[Arimura Jisaemon](/w/index.php?title=Arimura_Jisaemon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Arimura Jisaemon (page does not exist)") preparing to decapitate [Ii Naosuke](/wiki/Ii_Naosuke "Ii Naosuke") (1860)

The culmination of unrest among lower-*samurai* in response to the heavy-handed exercise of authority by the *bakufu* occurred when Ii [was assassinated](/wiki/Sakuradamon_Incident_(1860) "Sakuradamon Incident (1860)") in 1860. This action engendered a new violent consciousness centred on the principle of *sonnō jōi*. Later that same year, Tokugawa Nariaki died while still under confinement.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000295-87) Jansen writes that, in the aftermath of the killing, many lower and middle-ranking *samurai* began to see a means to effect "changes to their personal and collective position".[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994106-88) In Satsuma Domain, a loyalist group under [Ōkubo Toshimichi](/wiki/%C5%8Ckubo_Toshimichi "Ōkubo Toshimichi") moved towards conciliation with the domain officials, concerning themselves with persuading the *daimyō* to break with the *bakufu* cause; [Saigō Takamori](/wiki/Saig%C5%8D_Takamori "Saigō Takamori") would join this group upon return from his exile in 1862.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972157-89) In Tosa Domain, [Takechi Hanpeita](/wiki/Takechi_Hanpeita "Takechi Hanpeita") met with Kido Takayoshi and [Kusaka Genzui](/wiki/Kusaka_Genzui "Kusaka Genzui") who shared their martyred teacher's philosophy. Formalising his leadership over a group of local *shishi* in October 1861, the [loyalist party](/wiki/Tosa_Kinn%C5%8D-t%C5%8D "Tosa Kinnō-tō") (among whom was counted [Sakamoto Ryōma](/wiki/Sakamoto_Ry%C5%8Dma "Sakamoto Ryōma"), although he left in 1862[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994117-90)) did not view their loyalty to the Emperor as contradictory to their traditional feudal loyalties.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994108–109-91)

The *shishi* loyalists achieved some success in national politics, successfully renegotiating the conditions of *kōbu gattai* to favour the Imperial Court through their co-operation with the [Chōshū](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB_Domain "Chōshū Domain") government.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994130,_137–138-92) *Shishi* were promoted in these regions (Ōkubo in Satsuma Domain, and Kido in Chōshū—both of whom were seen to be a moderating force against extremists), but they did not represent a majority view of domain officials.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972187-93) Despite the politics of Chōshū Domain being decidedly more moderate than the advice of the Chōshū loyalists in Kyōto,[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972187-93) the regent of Satsuma Domain, [Shimazu Hisamitsu](/wiki/Shimazu_Hisamitsu "Shimazu Hisamitsu") was angry at the amount of influence Chōshū Domain *shishi* were having on Court politics.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994137–138-94)

#### Foreign intervention

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Foreign intervention")]

During a mission to Kyōto regarding the position of the Imperial Court in the political authority of the *bakufu* to order the expulsion of the foreigners, the retainers of Shimazu Hisamitsu's procession [killed a British merchant](/wiki/Namamugi_Incident "Namamugi Incident") who attempted to cross through the procession.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972183-95)[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994129-96) Subsequent British threats for satisfaction drew Shimazu's attention away from Court politics, and thus in March 1863, the Imperial Court issued the [order to expel barbarians](/wiki/Order_to_expel_barbarians "Order to expel barbarians").[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972190,_194-97) When the deadline for the order to expel the barbarians came, Chōshū Domain decided to [open fire](/wiki/Battle_of_Shimonoseki_Straits "Battle of Shimonoseki Straits") on the foreign powers in their waters, starting what became known as the [Shimonoseki campaign](/wiki/Shimonoseki_campaign "Shimonoseki campaign"). In August, British frustrations with Satsuma inaction regarding the recompense they were seeking boiled over, leading to the [bombardment of Kagoshima](/wiki/Bombardment_of_Kagoshima "Bombardment of Kagoshima").[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000314-98) The expulsion order coupled with military action led not only to a deterioration of relations with the foreign powers, but also issued a challenge to *bakufu* authority.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972198-99) The resultant military failure of Satsuma and Chōshū to repel the foreign powers led the Imperial Court to backtrack, affirming the administrative role of the *shōgun*. However, the announcements to strengthen the authority of the *bakufu* did so at the expense of the interests of the *daimyō* who sought to reform the structure of governance.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972202–203-100)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/ShimonosekiMap.jpg/330px-ShimonosekiMap.jpg)](/wiki/File:ShimonosekiMap.jpg)

Map showing the [allied attack of Shimonoseki](/wiki/Shimonoseki_campaign#Final_battle,_September_5–6,_1864 "Shimonoseki campaign") (September 1864)

The Shimonoseki agreement to end the foreign attacks signed by the *bakufu* in October 1864 signalled a shift in policy that prioritised dealing with the foreign powers in a nonantagonistic manner. The *bakufu* would marshal its influence against the Imperial Court whenever they agitated for a policy of *jōi*. Similarly, the burgeoning anti-Tokugawa movement was shifting ideological emphasis away from direct expulsion towards *fukoku kyōhei* (rich nation, strong army) as a way to deal with the threat posed by the foreign powers.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972207–208-101) Indicative of this is that, just prior to this time, Sakamoto Ryōma met Katsu Kaishū intending to assassinate him for his perceived pro-foreign beliefs, what occurred instead was a conversation wherein Ryōma became convinced by Kaishū's plan for rearmament, following Kaishū when he later established the [Kobe Naval Training Centre](/wiki/Kobe_Naval_Training_Center "Kobe Naval Training Center") in 1863.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994163,_166–167-102)[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972208–209-103) As *bakufu* power began to be challenged, a split emerged between the foreign powers; France, represented by [Léon Roches](/wiki/L%C3%A9on_Roches "Léon Roches") favoured bolstering the Tokugawa to deal with internal strife, whereas Britain's minister [Harry Parkes](/wiki/Harry_Parkes_(diplomat) "Harry Parkes (diplomat)") increasingly began to favour dealing with the southwest domains of Satsuma and Chōshū.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000315-104)

### Anti-Tokugawa alliance and domain rebellion, 1864–1867

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Anti-Tokugawa alliance and domain rebellion, 1864–1867")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Tenguto_no_ran.jpg/500px-Tenguto_no_ran.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tenguto_no_ran.jpg)

Japanese print by [Utagawa Kuniteru III](/w/index.php?title=Utagawa_Kuniteru_III&action=edit&redlink=1 "Utagawa Kuniteru III (page does not exist)") depicting "[Takeda Kōunsai](/wiki/Takeda_K%C5%8Dunsai "Takeda Kōunsai") at Mt. Tsukuba" under the banner of *[sonnō jōi](/wiki/Sonn%C5%8D_j%C5%8Di "Sonnō jōi")*.

A period of conservative reaction against the *shishi* followed the military failures of 1863 and 1864.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972226-105) It was during this period of backlash against the *sonnō jōi* ideology of the loyalists that Takechi Hanpeita was arrested and compelled to commit *[seppuku](/wiki/Seppuku "Seppuku")* by the *daimyō* of Tosa Domain Yamauchi Yōdō.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972218-106) Following the death of Tokugawa Nariaki in 1860, Mito Domain had been dominated by upper-ranking conservative *samurai* who favoured conciliation with the Tokugawa *bakufu*, but a power struggle developed from the belief that the *bakufu*'s pro-foreign tendencies were threatening the Imperial Court's expulsion edict. With news of uprisings in Yamato and Tajima, in May 1864 pro-*sonnō jōi* loyalists declared the [Mito Rebellion](/wiki/Mito_Rebellion "Mito Rebellion") in defiance of the *bakufu*.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972221–222-107)

Beasley identifies two principal lessons from the military defeat of the Mito Rebellion, the requirement of one of the great domains to support the movement and the division within the social structure of Restoration politics.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972223-108) The *shishi*, being predominantly lower-ranking *samurai* of the rural elite, were abandoned by the local peasantry (poorer farmers either abandoned or joined the *bakufu* forces in attacking the loyalists). Similarly, the tepid support for (in the case of Mito) or the criticism of (in Yamato and Tajima) the revolts of the lower-ranking *samurai* by the more moderate middle-ranking *samurai*, shifted the emphasis towards "a political method more in conformity with the needs of feudal society."[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972224-109) By the spring of 1864, Satsuma Domain policy had shifted to moderation, seeking the removal of *shishi* influence from the Court and its domain government, as part of this policy they worked with the *bakufu* to violently suppress the *shishi* in Kyōto. As a result, Chōshū became the last refuge for *sonnō jōi* loyalists. A power struggle over the domain's politics soon followed, as did a contest between *bakufu* and domain political power.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972214–215-110)

In Chōshū, the remnant *shishi* organised themselves into militias (e.g. the *[Kiheitai](/wiki/Kiheitai "Kiheitai")* led by Takasugi Shinsaku) to assist the domain's army in the event of attack by the *bakufu* or foreign powers. The militias supported the moderate [Sufu Masanosuke](/w/index.php?title=Sufu_Masanosuke&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sufu Masanosuke (page does not exist)") when he was forced from office by domain conservatives, and secured positions for other loyalists, including Takasugi and Kido Takayoshi, exerting their power over the government.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972226–229-111) When the Imperial Court issued edicts that reneged on the prior order to expel the barbarians, many in the militias wished to march on Kyōto to regain access to the Emperor.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972229–230-112) Despite opposition from Takasugi, the extremists won out, but the resulting [Hamaguri Gate Rebellion](/wiki/Kinmon_incident "Kinmon incident") was a political failure, leading to the death of Kusaka Genzui. The *bakufu* quickly moved to declare Chōshū Domain in rebellion, launching the punitive [First Chōshū expedition](/wiki/First_Ch%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB_expedition "First Chōshū expedition") with the co-operation of Satsuma Domain forces under Saigō Takamori in the autumn of 1864.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972231–232-113)

Despite the *rōju* advocating for the execution of Chōshū's *daimyō* [Mōri Takachika](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_Takachika "Mōri Takachika"),[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972250-114) the result of the settlement was relatively generous to Chōshū, due in part to the intervention of Katsu Kaishū and Saigō.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972232-115) As such, when the expedition disbanded, the Chōshū militias submitted a memorandum to the domain government chastising them for acquiescing to the *bakufu*'s demands. Then, in early 1865 Takasugi launched an attack on the domain government. The *Kiheitai* under [Yamagata Aritomo](/wiki/Yamagata_Aritomo "Yamagata Aritomo") joined the attack, and the domain government quickly ousted the conservatives who had been restored to power following the *bakufu*'s punitive expedition.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972233-116) The loyalists advocated for turning from a position of foreign expulsion to *fukoku kyōhei*, with [Itō Hirobumi](/wiki/It%C5%8D_Hirobumi "Itō Hirobumi"), [Inoue Kaoru](/wiki/Inoue_Kaoru "Inoue Kaoru") and [Ōmura Masujirō](/wiki/%C5%8Cmura_Masujir%C5%8D "Ōmura Masujirō") joining Takasugi and Kido in seeking to open the port of Shimonoseki for trade in order to import foreign weapons.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972238–239-117)

#### Satsuma-Chōshū Alliance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Satsuma-Chōshū Alliance")]

Main article: [Satchō Alliance](/wiki/Satch%C5%8D_Alliance "Satchō Alliance")

Beasley identifies three issues in Japanese politics until this point: that of foreign policy, that of Tokugawa authority, and that of feudal discipline. In reducing the importance of *jōi* sentinment in Chōshū, the first issue was no longer divisive; the incorporation or destruction of *shishi* loyalists by the domain governments across Japan had solved many problems related to the third issue; this left the question of *bakufu* power at the centre of national politics.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972241–242-118) Until this point, Satsuma Domain's cooperation with the *bakufu* in the Hamaguri Gate Rebellion and subsequent punitive expedition had been a point of tension and mistrust with Chōshū Domain.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994185-119) However, continued *bakufu* designs to destroy Chōshū pushed Saigō Takamori to reject the prerogatives of the Tokugawa, he appealed to the domain regent Shimazu Hisamitsu and began buying weaponry from the British.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994194–195-120) Meanwhile, Sakamoto Ryōma assisted Chōshū loyalists to bypass the *bakufu*'s prohibition on domain weapons trade by setting up connections to British merchants via his firm in Nagasaki. From here he laid the logistical and diplomatic foundations for co-operation between the two domains.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994217-121) On 7 March 1866, Sakamoto successfully brought together Kido Takayoshi and Saigō Takamori to formalise an alliance between Satsuma and Chōshū.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994221-122)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/%E8%96%A9%E9%95%B7%E5%90%8C%E7%9B%9F%E8%A3%8F%E6%9B%B8_%28Satch%C5%8D_Alliance_uragaki%29_by_Ryoma_Sakamoto.jpg/250px-%E8%96%A9%E9%95%B7%E5%90%8C%E7%9B%9F%E8%A3%8F%E6%9B%B8_%28Satch%C5%8D_Alliance_uragaki%29_by_Ryoma_Sakamoto.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E8%96%A9%E9%95%B7%E5%90%8C%E7%9B%9F%E8%A3%8F%E6%9B%B8_(Satch%C5%8D_Alliance_uragaki)_by_Ryoma_Sakamoto.jpg)

Note on the back of the [Satchō Alliance](/wiki/Satch%C5%8D_Alliance "Satchō Alliance") written by [Sakamoto Ryōma](/wiki/Sakamoto_Ry%C5%8Dma "Sakamoto Ryōma").

Planning for the [Second Chōshū expedition](/wiki/Second_Ch%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB_expedition "Second Chōshū expedition") sought to end the possibility of domain resistance to Tokugawa authority.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994213-123) When hostilities became inevitable by the summer of 1866, Chōshū moved quickly to repulse or forestall any *bakufu* operations.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972257-124) When the *bakufu* sent out requests among Chōshū's neighbouring domains to assist in shogunal efforts, the domains offered noncommittal or hostile responses. Many of the *daimyō* were suspicious of *bakufu* aims and were concerned about encroaching French influence in the *bakufu*'s capability to build a centralised state.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994213-123) Satsuma regent Shimazu Hisamitsu expressed a widely held belief in a memorial to the Imperial Court that accused the *bakufu* of seeking a draconian settlement with Chōshū that risked the safety of the country at large.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972258-125) The simultaneous death of *shōgun* Tokugawa Iemochi caused the *bakufu* to seek a truce.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972257-124)

#### Restoration movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Restoration movement")]

Shortly following the success of Chōshū against the *bakufu*, Court noble [Iwakura Tomomi](/wiki/Iwakura_Tomomi "Iwakura Tomomi") suggested the Emperor agitate openly for full imperial restoration,[[f]](#cite_note-126) but the Court remained cautious of overplaying their hand.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972260–262-127) At the same time, the *daimyō* had their own designs for restructuring the system of feudal autonomy that did not fully correspond with his proposal.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972262-128) Emperor Kōmei died in February 1867, leaving the teenager Mutsuhito to ascend to the throne as [Emperor Meiji](/wiki/Emperor_Meiji "Emperor Meiji").[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972286-129) Offers for a mediated settlement were proposed by the new *shōgun* Tokugawa Yoshinobu; but when they were ignored the *bakufu* instituted a series of reforms to the military, administration, and finance.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972262–263-130) These serious efforts at reform sparked worries among some in the anti-Tokugawa alliance, and in 1867, Saigō and Ōkubo Toshimichi both wrote to Shimazu to indicate their support for returning the country's administration to the power of the Emperor.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972266-131)

At the urging of Saigō and Ōkubo, four *daimyō* ([Date Munenari](/wiki/Date_Munenari "Date Munenari"), [Matsudaira Yoshinaga](/wiki/Matsudaira_Yoshinaga "Matsudaira Yoshinaga"), Yamauchi, and the regent Shimazu) sent to negotiate with the *shōgun* over the opening the port at Kōbe and the policy towards Chōshū repudiated the compromise they made with Tokugawa Yoshinobu. In late June, they publicly disputed the *shōgun*'s account in the Imperial Court when Tokugawa attempted to take advantage of a fractured opposition to assert the traditional authority of the *shōgun*.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972271–272-132)[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994292-133) As plans formalised among the anti-Tokugawa alliance, Sakamoto drafted an Eight Point Plan that would serve as the foundation of restoration politics.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994296-134) It included provisions for expanding the military, reform of the law, establishing a bicameral legislature, and the return of political power to the Imperial Court.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972276–277-135)[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000310-136) As a representative of Tosa Domain, Sakamoto was seeking to position himself between the Satchō alliance and the *bakufu* and submitted his proposals to the Tosa Domain government,[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972277-137)[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000310-136) which became the basis for plans submitted to the *shōgun* to persuade him to resign his powers.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994295-138)

## Imperial restoration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Imperial restoration")]

### Dissolution of the shogunate, 1867

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Dissolution of the shogunate, 1867")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Taisei_H%C5%8Dkan.jpg/250px-Taisei_H%C5%8Dkan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taisei_H%C5%8Dkan.jpg)

[Tokugawa Yoshinobu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Yoshinobu "Tokugawa Yoshinobu") informs officials of his decision at [Nijō Castle](/wiki/Nij%C5%8D_Castle "Nijō Castle") in 1867.

With the domains preparing to use military force to remove the *bakufu* government,[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994297-139) the Tosa Domain memorandum was submitted to Tokugawa Yoshinobu at a time when some of the *shōgun*'s advisors were advocating for similar reforms.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972279-140) Seeking to maintain his title and influence,[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994331-141)[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000311-142) on 8 November 1867, Tokugawa Yoshinobu, the 15th Tokugawa *shōgun*, decided to renounce the administrative functions of his office and returned the power to the Imperial Court.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972281-143) The following day, the Court received the memorandum, but also sent a rescript authorising the domain governments to use military force to oust the *bakufu*.[[g]](#cite_note-145)[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994331-141) Ten days later on 19 November, Tokugawa also resigned the title of *shōgun*. As Tokugawa remained inert in temporarily exercising his authority, the Court was divided, while the *daimyō* continued planning for a violent confrontation.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972283-146) Iwakura Tomomi, Saigō Takamori, and Ōkubo Toshimichi spent the months of November and December organising a [coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état") within the divided Imperial Court, with the aim to strip the Tokugawa house of their lands held in fief. This would prevent them from dominating the future council of *daimyō*.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994332–333-147)

The suspicion and disorder that accompanied the *shōgun*'s resignation led to violence in Kyōto, during which Sakamoto Ryōma was assassinated.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994335–336-148) Tosa Domain had sponsored the *shōgun*'s abdication request, but Satsuma and Chōshū officials were intent on establishing their domains at the centre of a new system.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000311-142) The requirement for Satsuma and Chōshū to demonstrate they were acting in accordance with the wishes of the Emperor led them to formulate the 3 January proclamation, making the Imperial Court's anti-shogunate stance official.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972290-149) In analysing how the Tokugawa shogunate collapsed so suddenly, Jansen indicates that the division of the *bakufu*'s network of feudal relations had its attention split between focus on the Imperial Court and individual preoccupation with domain affairs. The office of *shōgun* suffered an erosion of authority as domain bureaucrats began exercising their power over policy to promote their leadership over other domains'; this evolved into a programme realised by individual personalities and military force.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000331–332-150)

### January Proclamation, 1868

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: January Proclamation, 1868")]

In the early hours of 3 January 1868, domain troops seized control of the Imperial Palace from the *bakufu* while Iwakura simultaneously received official approval from the Emperor for these actions.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994333-151) Prior to a hastily summoned meeting organised by Iwakura, the Emperor granted permission to announce a decree that would accept Tokugawa Yoshinobu's resignation as *shōgun* and resume the political functions of the Imperial House. Among those in attendance were [Matsudaira Shungaku](/wiki/Matsudaira_Shungaku "Matsudaira Shungaku") and [Nakayama Tadayasu](/wiki/Nakayama_Tadayasu "Nakayama Tadayasu"), while adherents to the *bakufu* (including many Court officials) were prevented from attending.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972291-152) Tokugawa rule was thus ended by the Emperor's declaration of the restoration of his authority:[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen1994333-151)[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000334-153)

> The Emperor of Japan announces to the sovereigns of all foreign countries and to their subjects that permission has been granted to the *[Shōgun](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dgun "Shōgun")* Tokugawa Yoshinobu to return the governing power in accordance with his own request. We shall henceforward exercise supreme authority in all the internal and external affairs of the country. Consequently, the title of Emperor must be substituted for that of *[Taikun](/wiki/Taikun "Taikun")*, in which the treaties have been made. Officers are being appointed by us to the conduct of foreign affairs. It is desirable that the representatives of the treaty powers recognize this announcement.

— [Mutsuhito](/wiki/Mutsuhito "Mutsuhito"), January 3, 1868[[147]](#cite_note-154)

Despite the new government being in a state of precarity, public placards and [street demonstrations](/wiki/Ee_ja_nai_ka "Ee ja nai ka") were often loyalist in tone.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972292–293-155) The restructuring of the relationship between Court and *bakufu* that this pronouncement represented was materialised in the abolition of prior offices, to be replaced with a [Chief Executive](/wiki/S%C5%8Dsai "Sōsai") ([Prince Arisugawa](/wiki/Prince_Arisugawa_Taruhito "Prince Arisugawa Taruhito")), Senior Councillors, and Junior Councillors. Many of the first office holders were directly specified in the meeting, with Chōshū Domain remaining absent from receiving official placements as the *daimyō* had not yet been pardoned.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972291-152) Following the proclamation, the newly formed council was split over how to treat Tokugawa Yoshinobu. Tokugawa registered his intention to negotiate and decided to withdraw to [Ōsaka Castle](/wiki/%C5%8Csaka_Castle "Ōsaka Castle") on 6 January. This move to mediate between Court and *bakufu* temporarily weakened the position of Satsuma officials in the council.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972294-156) The pardoning and accession of Chōshū Domain officials rallied support and co-operation from other domains and loyalist groups.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972295-157) Both sides were becoming belligerent, some Satsuma *samurai* provoked riots against the Tokugawa in Edo. Upon hearing this, Tokugawa's loyal vassals persuaded him to take up arms against the council.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972295–296-158)[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000312-159)

### Boshin War, 1868–1869

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Boshin War, 1868–1869")]

Main article: [Boshin War](/wiki/Boshin_War "Boshin War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/02/Emperor_Receives_Foreign_Ministers_by_Hiroshima_K%C5%8Dho_%28Meiji_Memorial_Picture_Gallery%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emperor_Receives_Foreign_Ministers_by_Hiroshima_K%C5%8Dho_(Meiji_Memorial_Picture_Gallery).jpg)

[Emperor Meiji](/wiki/Emperor_Meiji "Emperor Meiji") receives Dutch minister [Dirk de Graeff van Polsbroek](/wiki/Dirk_de_Graeff_van_Polsbroek "Dirk de Graeff van Polsbroek") in 1868.

Recognising that the Court had made a mistake in allowing Tokugawa autonomy, Ōkubo contacted Iwakura and the Satchō military officers to take action before Tokugawa could re-establish his influence among the *daimyō*. On the 27 January, the [Battle of Toba–Fushimi](/wiki/Battle_of_Toba%E2%80%93Fushimi "Battle of Toba–Fushimi") took place, in which Chōshū and Satsuma's forces defeated the Tokugawa's army.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972296-160) Seeking to avoid a full [civil war](/wiki/Civil_war "Civil war"), Tokugawa retreated to Edo by sea and refused to counter-attack.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972296–297-161) An Imperial decree on 31 January blamed the Tokugawa for starting hostilities and Ōsaka Castle surrendered three days later. The army moved to take Edo during late February,[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972297-162) then on 1 March, Prince Arisugawa was made supreme commander of the Imperial forces during the campaign.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeene2002131-163)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Surrender_of_Edo_Castle_%28Meiji_Memorial_Picture_Gallery%29.jpg/250px-Surrender_of_Edo_Castle_%28Meiji_Memorial_Picture_Gallery%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Surrender_of_Edo_Castle_(Meiji_Memorial_Picture_Gallery).jpg)

[Saigō Takamori](/wiki/Saig%C5%8D_Takamori "Saigō Takamori") and [Katsu Kaishū](/wiki/Katsu_Kaish%C5%AB "Katsu Kaishū") negotiating the surrender of [Edo Castle](/wiki/Edo_Castle "Edo Castle").

The Court, seeking improved relations with the foreigners, issued orders that brought their military conduct in alignment with international standards, and on 23 March the Dutch minister [Dirk de Graeff van Polsbroek](/wiki/Dirk_de_Graeff_van_Polsbroek "Dirk de Graeff van Polsbroek") and the French minister Léon Roches became the first European envoys to receive a personal audience with Meiji.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeene2002132–133-164) Seeking a negotiated settlement, Tokugawa wrote to Saigō Takamori and elevated Katsu Kaishū to a position with the authority to represent the Tokugawa house. In April, Katsu and Saigō met to discuss the conditions that would be faced by Tokugawa. Both sides agreed to Tokugawa surrendering himself, his castle, and military force.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972297-162) Iwakura took this agreement and stipulated that Tokugawa Yoshinobu resign as head of the family and surrender all but 700,000 *koku* of his lands, a deal that Tokugawa accepted on 3 May.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972297–298-165)

Some Emperor loyalists were unsatisfied with what they considered to be lenient conditions, meanwhile, troops of the [Northern Alliance](/wiki/%C5%8Cuetsu_Reppan_D%C5%8Dmei "Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei") from [Aizu](/wiki/Aizu_Domain "Aizu Domain") and [Sendai](/wiki/Sendai_Domain "Sendai Domain") challenged the authority of Satsuma and Chōshū Domain exercised over the Emperor. The Alliance was defeated by early November, but remnants of the old *bakufu* navy under [Enomoto Takeaki](/wiki/Enomoto_Takeaki "Enomoto Takeaki") escaped to [Hokkaidō](/wiki/Hokkaid%C5%8D "Hokkaidō"), where they attempted to set up a breakaway [Republic of Ezo](/wiki/Republic_of_Ezo "Republic of Ezo"). Forces loyal to the Emperor ended this attempt in May 1869 with the [Battle of Hakodate](/wiki/Battle_of_Hakodate "Battle of Hakodate").[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972298-166) Seeking to end further hostilities and unify the country, Tokugawa Yoshinobu was pardoned on 1 November 1869, after which many of the Tokugawa loyalists (including Enomoto and [Nagai Naoyuki](/wiki/Nagai_Naoyuki "Nagai Naoyuki")) were also pardoned. This extension of clemency ended the antagonistic relationship between the Court and *bakufu*.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972298–299-167) The capital of Edo was renamed [Tōkyō](/wiki/Tokyo_Prefecture_(1868%E2%80%931943) "Tokyo Prefecture (1868–1943)") on 3 September 1868. The Emperor took up residence in the city in 1869.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972327-168)[[h]](#cite_note-169)

To Beasley, the result of the Boshin War reflected a victory of the political programme of the *shishi* and the modernising technological reforms of progressive *daimyō*. Until their victory over the Tokugawa, the supporters of the Imperial Court (chiefly Satsuma and Chōshū) were not seeking to change the institutions of power but displace those wielding it. The issues facing the new government sought conceptual and pragmatic solutions that led to the development of a centralised [absolutism](/wiki/Autocracy "Autocracy").[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972300–301-170) National unity brought about by political change was considered a necessary objective to fulfil by members of the new government.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972300-171)[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000334-153)

## Imperial reform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Imperial reform")]

Main articles: [Meiji period](/wiki/Meiji_period "Meiji period") and [Government of Meiji Japan](/wiki/Government_of_Meiji_Japan "Government of Meiji Japan")

### Charter Oath, 1868

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Charter Oath, 1868")]

Main article: [Charter Oath](/wiki/Charter_Oath "Charter Oath")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/5jo1.gif/250px-5jo1.gif)](/wiki/File:5jo1.gif)

The five clauses of the [Charter Oath](/wiki/Charter_Oath "Charter Oath") as promulgated in April 1868.

Drawn up by figures largely from the southwestern domains, the Charter Oath of 1868 was intended as a document to reassure non-Restorationist domains that they would be consulted about decisions concerning objectives of the government.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972322-172)[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen2000337-173) However, the final draft went further and directly indicated the policy objectives of the new Imperial government.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972322-172) The Oath was issued on 6 April 1868.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972323-174)[[i]](#cite_note-176) The provisions of the Charter Oath called for the rejection of past political administration, for the country to be enriched, and the adoption of Western technology.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972324-177)

The emphasis of the Charter Oath was on the confirmation of the Emperor's authority, harnessing national potential to confirm the new government. The Oath did not specify a progressive agenda or specific form of governance, but confirmed the new government under a system of centralised national sovereignty.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwale200963–64-178)

### Abolition of the Domains, 1868–1873

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Abolition of the Domains, 1868–1873")]

Main article: [Abolition of the han system](/wiki/Abolition_of_the_han_system "Abolition of the han system")

The abolition of the domains began from a desire to centralise authority and enforce political decisions within the multi-domain alliance that had brought the Restoration about.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972325–327-179) Starting in June 1868, the enforcement of some *bakufu* decrees concerning a prohibition on individual domains from forming alliances and issuing coinage were reinstated. The lands of the *shōgun* and his *daimyō* supporters were seized and reorganised into urban [prefectures](/wiki/Prefecture "Prefecture") (府, *fu*) and rural prefectures (県, *ken*), placing them under the authority of the new [Meiji government](/wiki/Government_of_Meiji_Japan "Government of Meiji Japan"). Other individual domains were initially left untouched, but were required to change their administration: to promote men of talent (thereby forcing the *daimyō* to give up much of their authority over their own domains) and send one representative to a consultative assembly in the new imperial capital.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972328-180)

Facing consternation from conservative *daimyō*, Kido Takayoshi and Itō Hirobumi agreed that Japan would require the *daimyō* to surrender their lands in order to create a centralised army and bureaucracy. To overcome these concerns, Itō drafted a proposal that provided access to titles, stipends, and positions in the new administration to those *daimyō* and their retainers who willingly surrendered their domains.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972329–330-181) Consulting with members of the domain governments, the mixed reception the proposal received pushed members of the Meiji government (including Ōkubo Toshimichi and [Itagaki Taisuke](/wiki/Itagaki_Taisuke "Itagaki Taisuke")), to force progress by acting unilaterally on behalf of their *daimyō*. The document submitted to the Imperial Court on 5 March 1869 put the lands of Satsuma, Chōshū, Tosa, and Hizen at the disposal of the Emperor.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972330–332-182)

The *daimyō* still remained broadly opposed to any plan that would cause them to cede their territory, many simply hoped to substitute the Emperor for the *shōgun*.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972333-183) Iwakura drafted a compromise proposal in July 1869, in it the *daimyō* were to retain their land under the title of governor or vice-governor. 10% of their domain revenues would be sent to the Court, with the rest spent on administrative costs, while they were to obey Imperial directives on matters of general policy. The upper strata was reformed to unify the *daimyō* and members of the Court into a new [aristocracy](/wiki/Kazoku "Kazoku").[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972334–335-184) In an attempt to shore up government support, Kido Takayoshi, [Sanjō Sanetomi](/wiki/Sanj%C5%8D_Sanetomi "Sanjō Sanetomi"), and Iwakura Tomomi sought to redress the imbalance of influence exerted by progressive reformers. Over the course of 1870 and 1871, they conducted missions to various *daimyō* of the former anti-*bakufu* alliance, and reached a collective decision that it was necessary to abolish the domains.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972343–344-185)

Domestic tensions within the domain alliance and the broader *samurai* class meant the debate over the abolition of the domains threatened both national unity and government unity.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972345-186) Kido, Saigō, and Ōkubo resisted this threat by re-organising the government to concentrate power among themselves. Kido and Saigō became the only two [Councillors](/wiki/Sangi_(Japan) "Sangi (Japan)") while Ōkubo became Minister of Finance.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972346-187) Through the promotion of [Ōkuma Shigenobu](/wiki/%C5%8Ckuma_Shigenobu "Ōkuma Shigenobu") and Itō Hirobumi to ensure the support of their respective domains, the *daimyō* in Tōkyō were summoned in August 1871, whereupon they were informed of the decision taken to abolish the domains without consultation. In return they were to keep a small portion of their income and gain access to the new aristocracy.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972347-188) These new aristocrats were ordered to move to Tōkyō, separated from their former domains, in January 1872, the 302 domains were formally reorganised into 72 [prefectures](/wiki/Prefectures_of_Japan "Prefectures of Japan").[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972349-189) According to Beasley, this process dissolved the feudal system even as a parallel social system arose to replace it, domain regional power began to exert control in the centralising bureaucracy as controllers of that bureaucracy, whereas before the regional governments had instead been "objects of [*bakufu*] policy."[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasley1972348-190)

### Abolition of the samurai class

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meiji_Restoration&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Abolition of the samurai class")]

The 1869 regulations on the allocation of domain revenues and the position of the *samurai* class largely undermined the power of hereditary position, as they had aimed to universalise the restructuring of the political and bureaucratic systems governing the domains. The promotion of efficiency and men of talent coincided with a severe cut to stipends that adversely affected the socioeconomic position of many lower and middle ranking *samurai* that had supported loyalist aims.
